data.title: TSMC to Lead Rivals at 2-nm Node, Analysts Say
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC will lead rivals Samsung and Intel for years in advanced semiconductor nodes with the recent launch of its 2-nm process, according to analysts surveyed by EE Times.

The Taiwan foundry’s initial 2-nm customers will include top chip designers like Nvidia, Apple, AMD, and Qualcomm, as well as hyperscalers like Microsoft, Amazon, and Google, according to the analysts.

Late in December, TSMC announced the production start of N2, the company’s term for 2 nm. N2 marks the first time for TSMC to adopt nanosheet transistor technology, also known as gate all around (GAA), years after foundry rival Samsung. Claiming full-node advances in performance and power consumption, TSMC developed low-resistance redistribution layer and super high-performance metal-insulator-metal capacitors to boost performance of the 2-nm node.

TSMC said its 2-nm tech will lead the chip industry in density and energy efficiency.

“With our strategy for continuous enhancements, N2 and its derivatives will further extend TSMC technology leadership well into the future,” the company said in a prepared statement.

TSMC’s Fab 20 and Fab 22 in Taiwan (shown above) are where 2-nm production is starting. (Source: TSMC)

The champion

Analysts agreed that TSMC will maintain its title as champion of the advanced nodes. The company will keep its most advanced tech on Taiwan, and that is a concern for some. The U.S. government has called the company’s advanced tech leadership a national security risk.

TSMC will have some 2-nm capacity in the U.S. by 2028, but leadership capacity and most advanced technology nodes will remain in Taiwan, International Business Strategies CEO Handel Jones told EE Times.

“TSMC’s N2 is looking like it will be one of the company’s greatest hits, with over 15 customers working on it,” TechInsights vice chair Dan Hutcheson told EE Times. “Publicly known customers with interest include Apple, Nvidia, Google, Broadcom, Marvell, AMD, Intel, and MediaTek.”

The analysts noted that design costs of up to $800 million for a new chip are a tiny part of wafer production costs reaching $20 to $30 billion per year for some TSMC customers. Chip designers in advanced nodes must make huge bets on foundries supplying wafers with high yield and on schedule.

TSMC has continued to execute its tech roadmap, providing customers sufficient capacity while its foundry rivals Samsung and Intel have struggled to deliver on promises for advanced-node production.

In October last year, Intel announced the ramp of its 18A GAA process, the company’s entrant against TSMC’s N2. Intel is using 18A to make its Panther Lake and Clearwater Forest processors at the company’s new Fab 52 in Arizona. Intel originally scheduled 18A for volume production by early 2025, with initial products like Panther Lake launching in 2025.

The gap between TSMC and the competition is widening rather than closing, Jones noted.

“It’s a big problem for the industry for there to be only one high-volume source for wafers and packaging,” Jones said.  

Samsung, which in 2022 became the world’s first chipmaker to adopt GAA in its fabrication process, stumbled with adoption of the new tech that is used to make 3D chips for AI processors.

“Samsung is still having issues, but now that Intel announced Panther Lake is in production, as the process matures, I expect Intel to gain some new foundry customers,” independent analyst Mike Demler told EE Times.

“For TSMC, 2 nm is its first GAA node, but it won’t have the backside power technology of Intel’s 18A,” Demler said. “TSMC will incrementally introduce improvements later in 2NP and 1.6 nm, then 1.4 nm. Collectively, those technologies will comprise the leading edge for the next three to five years.”

N2 will validate GAA even as it lacks backside power, Hutchison noted.

“The result is that while N2 makes great speed and power gains, its density gain is mediocre compared to [TSMC’s previous] N3E,” he said.

Competition

Intel and Samsung have failed to grab top customers from TSMC, which makes more than 90% of the world’s most advanced chips. TSMC’s position may slip for a variety of reasons, including geopolitics, Paul Triolo, China and Technology Policy Lead at DGA Group, told EE Times.

“The competition at the most advanced nodes here has become more complex with both Samsung and Intel, and now Rapidus in the mix,” Triolo said. “Intel and Samsung could have a shot in terms of capacity and supply assurance, multi-site redundancy, and geopolitical risk hedging—non-Taiwan based production—and commercial terms such as wafer pricing, engineering support and guaranteeing schedules.”

TSMC itself has recently built more fabs outside Taiwan, an island which could be in the crosshairs of a future conflict involving the U.S. and China.

Triolo notes that TSMC’s overall market share lead is “very large,” possibly exceeding 70%. From that base, competitor “inroads” are more likely to look like selective wins in high-value segments than a rapid displacement of TSMC’s overall dominance, he added.

“Intel can win meaningful share in U.S.-sovereign/hyperscaler custom silicon and some networking/ASIC niches if 18A ramps credibly and the ecosystem hardens—AWS-type programs are the template here,” Triolo said. “Samsung can win share via its U.S. fabs and anchor ramps. Here, Tesla/Apple supply relationships are the template, especially where customers explicitly want a second advanced-node source.”

Japanese startup Rapidus is a bit of a wildcard, he said.

Rapidus, which aims to start 2-nm production in 2027, could become a credible option for Japanese/Asia-based customers prioritizing supply chain sovereignty and specific advanced node programs they are willing to co-develop with the foundry, Triolo said.

TSMC appears to have reduced the probability of a Samsung-style GAA stumble by waiting longer to introduce GAA (first at N2 rather than N3), running an extended baseline/yield-enhancement program with early customer tape-outs, and now declaring on-schedule volume production, Triolo added.

“The only conclusive demonstration that the ‘hard parts’ are fully sorted is when multiple high-volume customer products, [mobile and HPC], ship on N2 with stable yields and predictable ramp rates through 2026,” Triolo said. “One area to watch is how SRAM and high-current blocks are behaving as this can be where early GAA pain can reveal itself, and also look at time from tape-out to high-volume manufacturing and whether there is significant slippage in schedules.”

Given TSMC’s track record, it seems likely that the company will cross these hurdles in 2026, he added.

“Expecting TSMC to stumble and provide openings for Intel and Samsung may not be the best bet,” Triolo concluded.

data.title: Imec Makes Solid-State Nanopores Using EUV Lithography
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

For more than two decades, solid-state nanopores have hovered on the edge of commercial relevance. Researchers have repeatedly demonstrated their promise for single-molecule sensing: detecting DNA, proteins, and other biomolecules by monitoring ionic current as they pass through nanometer-scale holes in a membrane. But despite thousands of academic papers, commercialization has remained elusive. The bottleneck was never proof of principle; it was manufacturing. That barrier may now be breaking.

In a recent demonstration, imec showed the first wafer-scale fabrication of solid-state nanopores using extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography, producing pores of roughly 10-nm diameter across full 300-mm wafers with high uniformity. More importantly, the work reframes nanopores not as artisanal devices drilled one by one with electron beams or focused ion beams but as manufacturable semiconductor structures, built with the same tools and process discipline used for advanced CMOS.

“This is really about showing that nanopores can be made with the same repeatability mindset as chips,” Pol Van Dorpe, associate professor of quantum solid-state physics at KU Leuven and fellow at imec, told EE Times in an exclusive interview. “Once it’s possible to make thousands, or eventually millions, of pores in one go, a wider group of people can build assays, instruments, and eventually products.”

Why EUV changes the equation

Until now, most solid-state nanopores were fabricated using e-beam lithography, focused ion beam drilling, or transmission electron microscopy sculpting. These techniques can produce exquisitely small pores, but they are fundamentally incompatible with volume manufacturing.

EUV lithography brings a different value proposition. Rather than carving pores individually, EUV enables simultaneous patterning of dense nanoscale features with nanometer-level dimensional control, wafer after wafer. “In lithography terms, EUV is actually the most precise step in the whole flow,” Van Dorpe said. “The remaining variability mostly comes from etch, membrane formation, and release—not from the lithography itself.”

Imec’s approach combines EUV patterning with a spacer-based shrink process and a dual-mask strategy. A dense EUV mask defines an array of ~20-nm apertures, while a second, lower-cost deep-UV mask selectively “closes” unwanted openings. This hybrid approach solves a subtle but critical mismatch: EUV tools are optimized for dense patterns, while nanopore applications often require sparse, isolated pores. “This technique gives us flexibility,” Van Dorpe said. “You don’t need a new EUV mask every time you want a different pore layout. You keep one high-density mask and change the cheaper one.”

Technically, the process resembles a MEMS flow more than a transistor flow. Two wafers are processed in parallel: one containing the nanopore membrane, the other providing backside fluidic access. The nanopore itself is formed in a thin silicon nitride membrane, temporarily filled with a sacrificial plug to protect it during subsequent processing. The wafers are then bonded, thinned, and released to expose the final pore.

The result is a mechanically robust membrane with a well-defined nanopore and integrated fluidic access—fabricated entirely at wafer scale. According to imec, pores down to approximately 10 nm were achieved across full 300-mm wafers, with good within-wafer and wafer-to-wafer uniformity.

But Van Dorpe cautions that this is not the end of the journey. “We showed what the patterning capability can bring,” he said. “Now the optimization continues, especially in etch and release steps, so that you consistently hit very tight-sized windows.”

To prove that the pores were not merely “pretty TEM images,” imec performed DNA translocation experiments at scale. Double-stranded DNA passing through the pores produced clear current blockades with signal-to-noise ratios that correlated strongly with pore size across the wafer. “That correlation is key,” Van Dorpe said. “It shows we’re not just making pores; we’re making sensors.”

From sequencing dreams to proteomics reality

Much of the early excitement around nanopores focused on DNA sequencing. Biological nanopores have since captured that market, thanks to atomic-scale size control and enzymatic ratcheting. Solid-state nanopores, Van Dorpe argues, should not try to replicate that path. “For single-base DNA sequencing, solid-state pores are not there—and maybe never will be,” he said. “But there are many applications where you don’t need angstrom-level resolution.”

Proteomics is one of the most compelling. Unlike DNA, proteins cannot be amplified, and their concentrations span many orders of magnitude. Measuring them at scale requires massive parallelization—precisely where wafer-scale solid-state pores shine. “If you want unbiased protein analysis, you need to count enormous numbers of molecules,” Van Dorpe said. “That’s where arrays of solid-state nanopores become very powerful.”

Imec is deliberately positioning this work as a platform rather than a finished instrument. Today, the organization offers single-pore and small multi-pore chips, packaging, fluidics, and reference readout electronics. A multi-pore instrument capable of measuring up to 96 pores in parallel is expected by mid-2026.

What imec is not providing is the biology. “The chemistry, the sample prep, the assay design—that has to come from partners,” Van Dorpe said. “It’s a co-optimization problem. The pore and the assay have to be developed together.”

That openness is intentional. Rather than locking partners into long, custom development cycles, imec is standardizing interfaces—fluidic, electrical, and mechanical—so life-science tool developers can experiment quickly.

Biological nanopores already ship in commercial systems, but they come with limitations: fragile membranes, sensitivity to harsh chemistries, and limited CMOS integration. Solid-state pores offer robustness and manufacturability but less molecular specificity. Van Dorpe sees a future where the two converge. “Docking biological pores into solid-state platforms is very attractive,” he said. “It’s more complex, but it could combine the best of both worlds.”

Asked what would convince skeptics that solid-state nanopores have crossed from “emerging” to “manufacturing,” Van Dorpe was pragmatic. “A partner product,” he said. “Something in the market that clearly benefits from wafer-scale fabrication—not just incrementally but fundamentally.”

With EUV now in play, solid-state nanopores no longer look like lab curiosities; they look like semiconductor devices—waiting for the right applications to catch up.

data.title: Higher-Current SiC Devices Shrink Locomotive Power Converters
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Power converter designers in rail traction are moving away from oversized, one-device-fits-all architectures. New SiC modules with higher current ratings allow motor drive and power conversion stages to be handled by separate devices, reducing oversizing and shrinking converter footprints.

Hitesh Bhardwaj, general manager and business head of Semiconductors and Devices at Mitsubishi Electric India, said the availability of 1,000 A and higher-current SiC devices is changing how converters are partitioned in high-horsepower locomotives.

One example is a newly designed, uniform full-SiC device rated at 800 A and 3.3 kV. “This is the first time an 800 A, 3.3 kV rating device has been designed by Mitsubishi Electric,” Bhardwaj said. The device is used inside power converters, with the exact configuration depending on customer design and system ratings.

Device placement and selection vary widely across railway applications. According to Bhardwaj, device choice changes with system power. “In railways, automotive systems have a certain power. For example, for 6,000 horsepower, we use this rating: 1,200 A, 4.5 kV. For a 12,000-horsepower design, we use 1,500 A, 3.3 kV, depending on the transformer rating.”

In mainline electric multiple units and diesel electric multiple units, 1,500 A, 3.3 kV devices are commonly used, alongside a newer 1,000 A, 4.5 kV rating. In newer 6,000 horsepower locomotive designs, manufacturers are shifting from a single-device approach to a combination of 1,500 A and 1,000 A devices. “In the earlier design, one device served both sides. Now the design uses a combination of these two devices,” Bhardwaj said.

Why designs are being split

Earlier traction architectures relied on a single, higher-rated device to handle multiple stages because lower-current options were not available.

“When the power came from the pantograph, it was fed to the transformers, then the rectifiers, followed by the DC-link where the capacitors sit, and fed to the inverter,” Bhardwaj said. “Because a 1,000 A rating was not available, the same device was used even for driving the motors, which was highly overpowered.”

With the introduction of a 1,000 A, 4.5 kV SiC device, motor drive stages no longer need oversized components. “Now we have the 1,000 A, 4.5 kV device, which participates in the converter leg or module,” he said.

Higher current density has also reduced module size. Bhardwaj said the industry-standard package of 140 mm × 190 mm has been reduced to 130 mm × 140 mm.

“More power and more current are packed into it,” he said. “Current density is much higher in the smaller device, so converter size is reduced, leaving more space in the locomotives.”

Smaller intelligent power modules in DIP packages

While the high-current SiC modules are targeted at traction and high-power conversion systems, Mitsubishi Electric is addressing a separate set of requirements in consumer appliances through lower-power intelligent power modules.

The company has also introduced SlimDIP modules using an RC IGBT structure, which integrates the IGBT and diode into a single chip. “Normally, the IGBT transistor and diode were separate,” Bhardwaj said. “In this compact DIP design, we combined them into a single chip.”

This integration reduced device size by53% without reducing power, according to the company. The SlimDIP devices have been launched in 30 A, 600 V and 50 A, 600 V ratings.

“This also improves thermal management,” Bhardwaj said. “The smaller printed circuit board assembly helps engineers integrate it into their designs.”

Mitsubishi Electric positions both silicon-based RC IGBT modules and SiC MOSFET-based SlimDIP modules for consumer appliances like air conditioners, washing machines and refrigerators. The differences lie in material and performance, particularly losses and thermal behavior.

Market adoption

The efficiency gains promised by SiC depend on the material, device packaging and system integration. While SiC offers lower switching and conduction losses than silicon, packaging parasitics and thermal constraints can reduce real-world efficiency gains.

When it comes to market adoption, cost also remains a key factor in technology choice, especially for a price-sensitive market like India.

“SiC is more expensive than silicon-based devices,” Bhardwaj said, adding that most five-star rated (energy efficiency regulation metric) consumer appliances continue to use silicon-based intelligent power modules (IPM) or dual-inline package IPM solutions.

Future efficiency regulations could shift that balance. “We hope that in the coming years, the Indian government may require six-star or seven-star ratings, achievable only with SiC-based devices,” he said.

Bhardwaj pointed out that Indian consumer electronics designers have traditionally followed established designs. “Until now, the mentality has been to follow what already exists. Innovation was limited earlier,” he said, adding that awareness of SiC technology is growing with electric vehicles leading, followed by consumer electronics globally.

In India, however, railways are the key driver for high-voltage products. “We supply to private players who make converters and then supply to locomotive manufacturers,” he said.

Mitsubishi Electric’s India operations focus on sales and application support rather than design. “In India, we only handle sales and marketing,” Bhardwaj said. “We help product designers choose the right product for their application.”

The company operates evaluation labs in Gurugram and Bengaluru to support customers with design challenges. Customization is possible but limited. “If requested, we can adjust small electrical parameters,” he said.

Mitsubishi Electric has invested in an 8-inch SiC wafer plant in Kumamoto Prefecture in Japan. Bhardwaj expects SiC and silicon to coexist initially. “Initially, they will operate in parallel, but eventually SiC will supersede silicon,” he said.

See also:

Silicon Carbide Growth Pivots Around AI and Energy

‘MUSiC’ Fab Aims To Be National Sandbox for Silicon Carbide (SiC)

SiC Modules, Converter Designs Come into Focus at PCIM India Debut

data.title: CES 2026 Consumer Tech Trends: EMEA Will Lead Growth
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Las Vegas, Nev. — At CES 2026 in Las Vegas, the Consumer Technology Association (CTA) opened the show’s preview day with its annual tech trends presentation, projecting that global consumer technology sales for 2025 will reach $1.3 trillion. While overall consumer spending growth is expected to be flat in 2026, the EMEA is forecast to drive growth.

However, we should not get too carried away: The research points to modest growth in the EMEA region, compared with flat performance in North America and a decline in China (see chart below). The CTA said the outlook underscores the industry’s resilience at a pivotal moment, as companies navigate economic challenges, shifting supply chains, and growing pressure on consumer spending.

Interestingly, if you dig into the data that Nielsen IQ (NIQ) Market Intelligence published, a key growth area will be small domestic appliances (SDAs). NIQ said consumers are ready to spend on SDA products that offer more convenience, more performance, or more versatility—even at higher prices. Multifunctionality and space efficiency remain big draws. But, as in other sectors, China’s 2025 trade-in policies create a difficult 2026 comparison, which will weigh down both domestic and global SDA figures.

Ines Haaga, director for global strategic insights at NIQ, said in the market researcher’s analysis, “Global growth in 2026 is a story of contrasts. Europe and MEA are accelerating as consumers trade up for premium and connected living, but always with value for money in mind. China, on the other hand, is cooling, after its trade-in policy-fueled surge. Success will come to brands that pivot fast: aligning pricing, innovation, and experience to regions where demand is rising and budgets matter.”

Add to this the CTA’s emphasis that even as tariffs and broader economic pressures intensify, Americans would continue to invest in technology that improves productivity, connectivity, and quality of life.

This is important as we talk about the growth of connected intelligent devices, which is part of the edge AI story. It shows where edge AI products could impact most and potentially create volume and revenue growth for the industry.

In its presentation, the CTA talked about three megatrends shaping the next phase of the consumer technology industry, all of which are on display at CES 2026:

  • Intelligent transformation: AIis becoming foundational across devices, platforms, and services, enabling smarter systems and more personalized consumer experiences.
  • Longevity technologies: Advances in digital health, remote care, and wellness tools are accelerating as consumers seek technologies that support longer, healthier lives.
  • Engineering tomorrow: Continued investment in electrification, mobility, energy management, and infrastructure modernization is laying the groundwork for future innovation.

We explored some of these technologies and discussed the trends on the show floor at CES Unveiled 2026 on Sunday night in Las Vegas. You can watch the full video of the conversation below:

data.title: Defense Dollars Drive Canadian Dual-Use Development
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Canada is amping up defense spending, but there are different views within the country’s semiconductor industry on how best to stake out its place in the supply chain.

One path is to build technologies with a defense-first mindset, but the process for getting technologies vetted and certified can be long and onerous. For smaller semiconductor companies looking to scale quickly, the procurement process is not easy to navigate. In addition, many companies do not offer a full solution but are looking to insert themselves into a broader stack that is not solely Canadian-owned.

The other path is to repurpose technologies developed for other industries for defense applications, an approach that exemplifies “dual use.”

Many semiconductor technologies developed to handle the extreme hot and cold conditions experienced by modern vehicles can be repurposed for defense and aerospace—even space applications, such as low-earth orbit satellites. Advanced technologies developed for defense and military applications frequently trickle down as far as the consumer level over time.

No matter the path taken or the amount of government funding and support, getting more Canadian semiconductor content into technology platforms at home or abroad will demand that new inroads are made into global supply chains and mindset shifts regarding procurement.

Exploring dual-use synergies

How technologies developed for harsh environments can be leverage for defense and how Canadian companies can better engage with government in defense procurement discussions was threaded throughout several panels at Accelerated, Canada’s Semiconductor Symposium, in late November.

Avinash Persaud, VP of ventureLAB’s Hardware Catalyst Initiative and Semiconductor Initiatives, said the conversations his organization has had with Canada’s Department of National Defense suggests that it is interested in “push opportunities” (i.e., they want to be made aware of technologies they did not know they could leverage).

“This may a great opportunity for us to get to the table,” Persaud said. “We’ve got something that you may not know you need.”

He added that there is also a push to buy Canadian first, with an emphasis on suppliers in the province of Ontario first. “This is now being driven towards us.”

Jean-Francois Bousquet, principal investigator at Dalhousie University’s Microelectronics Innovation, Design and Integration Hub in Halifax, also sees synergies between technologies developed for defense with those that have a benefit for broader society.

He said heterogeneous technology integration is critical for ocean and marine technologies—everything from battery requirements to high-data processing. “We’re pushing the edge.”

Robust marine technologies could be leveraged for defense, including surveillance applications that could help to ensure Canada’s arctic sovereignty.

Michel Dion, CEO at Strix, said the challenge for Canadian companies is that the country’s military is not a large customer. Redeveloping the defense industry could take several decades, so taking a dual-use approach is essential. “This is a way to rebuild some capability in Canada.”

It is not a silver bullet, he said, but it is starting conversations around defense that, until recently, no one wanted to have. “It needs to start with the military requirements. It needs to be top down.”

Another challenge is that government entities have different mandates, according to Dion. “One is to develop the industry; one is to develop capability.”

Funding taps are flowing

Canada’s federal government does want homegrown companies to supply defense equipment and technology.

The government recently launched a CDN $357.7 million (U.S. $261 million) Regional Defense Investment Initiative (RDII), delivered through regional development agencies across the country. In the country’s most populous province, FedDev Ontario will oversee nearly CDN $200 million (U.S. $146 million) in allocation to help companies integrate into domestic and international defense supply chains, advance dual-use technologies and bolster Canada’s ability to respond to global security needs.

The Atlantic Canada Opportunities Agency (ACOA) will also allocate funds. In an online information session, Mélanie LeBlanc, ACOA program officer, said the RDII aims to help businesses scale up and boost productivity, as well as expand production capacity, modernize equipment or adopt technologies to meet defense standards.

RDII also aims to support innovation and collaboration through research and ecosystem projects. “That could mean setting up or strengthening innovation centers or testing facilities or networks that help companies integrate into the defense supply chains.”

LeBlanc said RDII money could be invested in strategic regional assets, small scale or dual-use infrastructure like logistics, manufacturing or transportation projects that improve regional readiness. “The key is a clear connection to Canada’s defense.”

She said a university research center developing secure microelectronics that meet military specifications would qualify for RDII funding. “If you’re working in advanced manufacturing, clean tech, AI, data analytics or material science, and your technology could have a defense or security application, that’s what we call dual use.”

The concept of dual-use technologies is not new nor are efforts to fund them.

CMC Microsystems, a Canadian not-for-profit organization that provides tools and training for the country’s semiconductor ecosystem, has been out in front drumming up interest in DUET (Dual Use and Emergency Technologies), which would create a pan-Canadian research and development network to fund innovative, cost-effective solutions for military or civilian threats.

The proposed network would encompass academic groups, small-and-medium enterprises and large multinationals. While the intent of DUET is to be versatile and provide rapid responses with advanced technologies, preferably made-in-Canada, there may be technologies available in other countries that add value to the projects, so foreign partners will be encouraged.

In an interview with EE Times, DUET CEO Gord Harling said DUET follows a similar approach to the recently launched FABrIC program, which CMC spearheaded and manages.

DUET reflects Harling’s belief that Canadian semiconductor companies should not focus on developing defense technologies first. Instead, he sees other sectors, such as emergency services and response, as the ideal proving ground to demonstrate that a solution is viable and scalable.

Harling noted that emergency services and defense face similar challenges around communication and navigation. Technologies used to detect underwater drones attempting to sink a ship could also inspect underwater infrastructure for civilian emergencies. “If all the cell towers are down, how do we communicate? Maybe it’s stringing fibers with drones, or maybe it’s having a drone fly up there and act as a cell tower or repeater,” he speculated.

Dual-use technologies regional dualling

Other organizations actively looking to leverage Canada’s renewed interest in defense spending include Invest Ottawa, which in late October last year hosted defense experts and leaders from industry, academia, government and the investment community to outline a strategy to leverage the national capital region’s defense assets, talent and technology expertise.

The goal is to solidify Ottawa-Gatineau, a region that straddles the border between Ontario and Quebec, as Canada’s National Defense Innovation Hub to drive new investment and create more sovereign companies, IP, infrastructure and capabilities. The region is already home to 330 defense companies and four NATO Defense Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic Test Centers.

Such a hub would advance dual-use technologies by leveraging the strengths of the region, which include AI, quantum, cybersecurity, semiconductors and photonics.

Other regions in Canada also see themselves as strategic players as the country ramps up defense spending.

Ian Smith, director of business development at the University of Alberta’s faculty of engineering in Edmonton, said Alberta is well-positioned as one of the largest manufacturing hubs in North America with its focus on energy services.

It also has companies spun out of the university’s NanoFAB that could contribute to the dual-use innovation, he said in an interview with EE Times. “How do we then reposition this ecosystem to support national defense to really translate those defense investment dollars into jobs in Canada?”

Smith said increasing the number of Canadian defense contractors is incredibly difficult given that the country is heavily dependant on the U.S. “The supply chains are already foundationally interconnected.”

He said there is an opportunity to expand supply chain relationships for OEMs, contract equipment manufacturers and component manufacturers. “That’s where a lot of the photonics pieces would fall in and the semiconductors fall in.”

Smith said Canada has a strong talent pool that excels at research and foundational technology. “There’s this gap of handing it off to industry or giving industry the opportunity to commercialize, then there’s the gap in connecting to customers.”

As much as Canada wants to buy Canadian, he said, the customer is often a multinational looking to integrate components into a stack that is part of a final product—a drone, for example.

Building a system where Canadian semiconductor companies can contribute to defense requires trust and security, Smith said. “Companies now need to innovate within a trusted environment.”

A recent initiative that could advance this environment is the Defense Innovation Secure Hub, which aims to accelerate the development, integration and transition of dual-use technologies into sovereign defense and security outcomes, while safeguarding sensitive research and intellectual property.

Smith said this helps to signal to universities and companies what research they should advance. “Within this we’ve built a cycle of where we’re having the customer up front. We’re able to apply the research and those resources appropriately.”

If issues like trust, security and IP are addressed up front, things can move faster, he said. “Having these conversations upfront makes a huge difference in our ability to go quickly and understand the challenges within the system.”

Smith added that Canada is on the edge of procuring the most defense equipment in the next five to 10 years since World War II. “That scale needs to be appreciated.”

And there is a finite window of opportunity of investment develop capacities within Canada, he said. “We need to do it and get out of our own way sooner than later.”

data.title: The Great Memory Stockpile
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The global semiconductor industry is going through major changes, which analysts call the “Great Memory Pivot.” As 2026 begins, the market is splitting in two: the AI-driven infrastructure sector is booming, while the consumer electronics market is struggling with shortages and rising prices.

This divide is caused by a big shift in how silicon wafer capacity is used. High-margin AI components, especially High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), are taking up space that would normally go to chips for laptops, smartphones, and game consoles.

Economics of the supercycle

For the world’s leading memory manufacturers—SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, and Micron Technology—the artificial intelligence boom has triggered a “supercycle” of profitability that prioritizes value over volume.

After dealing with too much inventory in 2023, these companies have shifted their production to focus on HBM and enterprise-grade DDR5, which are essential for generative AI models.

The financial reasons for this shift are strong. PC memory modules make very little profit, but advanced server-grade modules now have profit margins as high as 75%, higher than many AI accelerators. As a result, the top three manufacturers have effectively sold out their HBM capacity for the year. Micron alone expects its gross margin to reach 68% in Q2 2026.

This shortage is not just about demand; it’s also about how chips are made. Industry data shows that HBM3E uses about three times as much wafer space as standard DDR5, due to larger chip sizes and lower production yields from vertical stacking.

Since there’s a limit to how many wafers can be made and it takes years to add more, every wafer used for AI memory means fewer are available for regular chips. This has led to shortages for companies like HP, Dell, and Lenovo.

“This is a zero-sum game: every wafer allocated to an HBM stack for an Nvidia GPU is a wafer denied to the LPDDR5X module of a mid-range smartphone or the SSD of a consumer laptop,” said analysts at IDC.

Consumer electronics more expensive in 2026

This shift in spending is likely to cause higher prices for consumers and corporate IT departments. For more than 10 years, the tech industry has relied on cheaper memory to improve devices without raising prices, but that time seems to be ending.

According to the Financial Times, executives at major PC manufacturers are sounding the alarm. Jeff Clarke, Chief Operating Officer of Dell Technologies, warned investors that the company has “never seen costs move at the rate” they are currently rising. Similarly, Winston Cheng, CFO of Lenovo, the world’s largest PC maker, admitted the company is now “stockpiling memory chips and other critical components” to buffer against shortages.

This stockpiling makes the shortage worse and pushes prices even higher. Analysts say average DRAM prices could rise by 50% to 55% in early 2026, and consumer electronics prices could rise by up to 20% as manufacturers pass on the extra costs.

The table below, based on industry supply chain reports, shows how the market is expected to split in 2026:

The AI PC paradox

The industry faces what’s called the “AI PC paradox.” Manufacturers want to sell new computers that can run AI models locally to boost sales. But these computers need much more memory—often 16GB to 32GB—to work well. The same trend that creates demand for these PCs is also making it harder to get the memory needed to build them.

As memory costs rise to as much as 18% of a PC’s total production cost, some modular computer brands have stopped selling individual components to stop hoarding. This has slowed down the upgrade cycle. IDC predicts PC shipments could drop by 9% in 2026, as expensive parts make “AI PCs” too costly for many consumers and businesses.

Desperate measures and geopolitical shifts

The rush to secure capacity has led to unexpected partnerships and increased geopolitical tension. As reported by Majeed Ahmad at EE Times, U.S.-based Micron Technology is discussing a possible deal with Taiwan’s Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corp (PSMC). Since Micron’s own factories are full, it wants to use PSMC’s 12-inch fab capacity to meet high demand. This shows how far companies are willing to go to find more production space.

At the same time, China is trying to capitalize on the shortage. ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), the country’s biggest memory maker, is planning a $4.2 billion IPO to expand its capacity. While Western companies focus on high-end AI products, CXMT aims to supply the regular memory market, which could change prices for older DDR4 and LPDDR5 chips.

However, geopolitical tensions complicate matters. Chinese tech companies like ByteDance and Alibaba are also stockpiling chips. ByteDance plans to spend almost $23 billion on AI infrastructure in 2026, buying chips now to protect against possible future regulations. This early buying makes the global shortage worse by taking chips off the market that would otherwise go to other Asian electronics companies.

Another supply chain chaos

The year 2026 could be a turning point for the semiconductor supply chain. The industry is changing its priorities, with the value of each wafer now based more on how it supports AI model training than on how many consumer products it can make.

For large companies like Microsoft and Meta, signing billion-dollar supply deals is the price of staying competitive in AI. For consumers, the days of cheap and plentiful memory are over.

The “AI tax” is now a real cost, not just in software subscriptions but also in the parts that go into every laptop and smartphone worldwide. As more silicon is used for data centers, our personal devices end up costing more.

Author’s note: This article is based on publicly available information and does not constitute financial or investment advice.

data.title: Two Events Illustrate How AI is Rewriting the Memory Book
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

While AI captured much of the technology limelight in 2025, an industry intrinsically tied to the AI bandwagon is also making its presence felt at the start of the new year. DRAM makers building high-bandwidth memory (HBM) devices prevalent in AI data centers are scrambling for fab capacity, and geopolitical tensions are making it only more challenging.

Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra anticipates that memory markets will remain tight beyond 2026; more notably, he expects Micron to be able to meet only about half to two-thirds of demand from several key customers this year. Micron and SK hynix also disclosed that they have already sold out their HBM inventory for 2026.

And while DRAM suppliers are desperately seeking new fab capacity to meet the insatiable demand for AI, data centers, and other high-density applications, what about consumer devices such as PCs, smartphones, and graphics? The semiconductor memory industry is clearly in a state of flux, and the following developments at the close of 2025 reflect a sense of paranoia and shifting grounds.

Micron talking to PSMC

The latest media reports affirm earlier talk about Micron evaluating a potential acquisition of the 12-inch fab of Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corp. (PSMC). This deal could potentially include a strategic alliance and technology cooperation. PSMC is a Taiwan-based pure-play foundry specializing in the fabrication of memory chips.

PSMC has recently completed its Tongluo fab in Miaoli, Taiwan; it has been designed with memory manufacturing processes as its core focus. There have been media reports that both Micron and SanDisk are seeking close cooperation with PSMC because their own fabs have maxed out. Memory suppliers like Micron are also seeking additional capacity amid increasing demand and rising memory prices.

According to Taiwan’s Economic Daily News, Micron and PSMC are contemplating three potential cooperation models. While the first one is built around a pure fab model, meaning a full-fledged fab acquisition, the second one entails technology transfer plus equipment relocation. The third option relates to a distribution model in which PSMC will retain a portion of the memory wafers it produces for its own sales.

The next memory story comes from China, where a DRAM underdog has started making waves by carving out a new path at a time when memory shortages are rocking the semiconductor industry.

CXMT’s quest for 3+1 era

ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), China’s largest manufacturer of memory chips, is preparing for an IPO in Shanghai to raise nearly $4.2 billion to expand wafer capacity, bolster fabrication technology, and increase investment in the development of next-generation DRAM and HBM devices. The timing of this move is no coincidence: the semiconductor industry is experiencing severe DRAM shortages in AI systems as well as consumer segments such as PCs and smartphones.

CXMT—which ranks number four in the global DRAM market behind Samsung, SK hynix and Micron—was founded in 2016 with state backing. According to market research firm Omdia, CXMT held 4% of the global DRAM market share in the second quarter of 2025. Moreover, the Hefei, China-based memory supplier doubled its year-on-year revenue in 2025 and is expected to swing to profitability in 2026.

The year 2026 could thrust this DRAM maker into the memory industry mainstream; CXMT could even become a disruptor if it changes the calculus of DRAM pricing. Though like its three larger rivals—Micron, Samsung, and SK hynix—CXMT is likely to focus on strategic segments such as AI rather than flooding the consumer market with cheap DRAMs.

An eventful year

CXMT has also been in the news recently over a technology espionage story involving Samsung engineers allegedly passing along 10-nm DRAM process technology to the Chinese memory company. South Korean authorities have arrested 10 Samsung engineers from memory research and design divisions suspected of leaking highly sensitive manufacturing know-how via handwritten documents and encrypted communication tools.

That includes allegations that a former Samsung engineer passed on sub-10nm DRAM technology to CXMT through handwritten notebooks compiled over nearly five years. It encompassed notes of more than 600 optimized process steps, including gas flow ratios, lithography photoresist settings, and reactor pressures.

These are the latest developments in the memory technology realm, and they could be a harbinger of a year full of action for the DRAM industry. The AI workloads will continue to heavily influence DRAMs and their specialized variant, HBM, bringing a pivotal shift to the memory industry, already known for its hyper-cyclical nature.

Micron’s $200 Billion Plan to Reshore U.S. Production Faces Challenges

Breaking Through Memory Bottlenecks: The Next Frontier for AI Performance

data.title: Cyient Semiconductors to acquire Kinetic Technologies for $93 million
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Cyient Semiconductors has signed a definitive agreement to acquire a majority stake in California-based Kinetic Technologies for a total consideration of up to $93 million.

The transaction is subject to customary closing conditions and is expected to close in the coming months.

The CEO of Cyient Semiconductors, Suman Narayan, told EE Times that this acquisition allows Cyient Semiconductors to expand its power application-specific standard product (ASSP) portfolio, while also gaining access to Kinetic’s patents and long-standing customer relationships.

He clarified that Cyient Semiconductors is not positioning itself as a broad standard products supplier. “We want to build ASSPs for markets such as data centers, electrification, automotive, networking, industrial automation, and emerging edge artificial intelligence compute workloads, and service large customer requirements, rather than generic catalog parts,” he said, pointing to the differentiation as the company builds scale.

Founded in 2006, Kinetic Technologies designs power conversion, display power, protection, and interface solutions, and holds more than 100 patents related to power and protection architectures. Its portfolio is largely centered on DC-DC power conversion and power protection functions, with products tailored to the specific requirements of the markets it serves. The company will retain its current leadership and engineering team, along with the organizational structure post-acquisition.

On production volumes, Narayan said Cyient Semiconductors is already shipping between five and ten million ASIC units annually. He said shipments will continue through 2025 and 2026, including multi-million-unit volumes, with Kinetic’s ecosystem helping improve cost competitiveness.

Broader business strategy

Narayan explained that Cyient Semiconductors integrates multiple standard ICs into a single custom ASIC when customers want better power efficiency, smaller form factors, or cost optimization. “In some cases, customers also want to integrate their own customized IP blocks, depending on application needs. Design engineers can also combine standard parts with custom logic,” he said.

As an example, Narayan pointed to work with Azimuth, where the customer’s proprietary IP was integrated with Cyient Semiconductors’ existing IP blocks.  “These are typically mid-tier customers looking to differentiate their products in the market,” he said.

With custom ASICs, the company is focusing on mid-tier customers that need a dependable partner, higher levels of integration, and support for low-volume production runs. These customers may have custom intellectual property to integrate.

Asked about challenges, Narayan said the hardest trade-off has been deciding where to allocate limited resources, particularly talent. “Finding strong ASIC and application-specific standard product talent and bringing them into an Indian company is our main focus,” he said.

“Long-term growth in semiconductors depends on consistently reinvesting about 12 to 15 percent of revenue into R&D, particularly for smaller companies, alongside cost control and ownership of IP,” he elucidated.

Responding to a hypothetical scenario in which the current strategy did not meet expectations, Narayan said the first adjustment would be market focus. “Within power, you can play in industrial, automotive, or medical segments. Today, we are primarily focused on industrial. We could expand into other segments to increase our addressable market,” he said.

“The second option would be to expand the custom ASIC footprint beyond 130 nanometers and look at 40 to 65 nanometers. That comes with higher cost and risk. You need the risk appetite for that,” he added.

Partnership-led expansion

Over the last year Cyient Semiconductors has announced multiple partnerships with major semiconductor players across the globe. Be it partnering with MIPS, or Global Foundries, or bringing in Jaswinder Ahuja, who is the former VP and India MD of Cadence Design Systems, to its board of advisors — Cyient Semiconductors has been shaping its broader strategy around sustained investment in research and development, tighter control of intellectual property, and partnerships across the semiconductor ecosystem.

Narayan also alluded to the company’s selection for the ₹4,500 crore (around US$500 million) contract to upgrade the Semiconductor Laboratory (SCL) fab in Mohali, Punjab, as part of the India Semiconductor Mission. The company has been selected to supply and qualify technology IPs (RF-CMOS, BCD, CMOS image sensor) for SCL’s 8-inch manufacturing line.

Referring to its partnership with Navitas Semiconductor to co-develop GaN products for India’s high voltage, high power market segments, Narayan said, “The plan is to move assembly and test to India, support power modules, and eventually build controllers and drivers tuned specifically for Navitas’ gallium nitride.”

The companies will build digital and mixed signal ICs, GaN based system modules and design enablement platforms targeting different segments including AI data centers, electric mobility, performance computing, energy grid infrastructure and industrial electrification. Cyient Semiconductors is likely to leverage its portfolio of power supply design, protection, sensing, and monitoring capabilities drawn from across Kinetic and Ansem for this partnership.

data.title: Canada Focuses Funding on Photonics and Quantum
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Accelerated, Canada’s national semiconductor symposium, provided a stage to showcase some of the first recipients of funding from FABrIC, a five-year CDN $223 (U.S. $163 million) initiative to grow the semiconductor industry in Canada.

Managed by CMC Microsystems, which also hosted the conference, the funding program put out a call for applications in September 2024. The first round of FABrIC Challenge Projects was announced in June 2025, allocating a total of CDN $35.6 million (U.S. $26 million) in total investment, including CDN $13.4 million (U.S. $9.8 million) in funding provided by Innovation, Science and Economic Development Canada.

This round and each subsequent round of FABrIC Challenges will have specific areas of focus. The first call for applications had two streams.

The first stream requested projects that sought to develop and commercialize new advanced sensors and other semiconductor products for strategic end sectors. The second stream requested fabrication processes for developing semiconductor manufacturing capabilities in strategic core technologies, including photonics, MEMS, quantum technologies like computing, and compound semiconductors to make them widely accessible across Canada. 

Photonics was well represented at the Accelerated project presentation session, which showcased several winners out of 20.

Among them was Ranovus Inc., a high profile photonics player founded in Ottawa, that also has offices worldwide. Mike Sekerka, the company’s VP and general manager of the company’s defense and government business unit, said Ranovus has a strong R&D focus on developing multi-wavelength laser test chips for large-scale applications, including programs for the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency.

He said FABrIC funding is helping Ranovus develop a showcase of Canadian technology to meet the global requirements for co-packaged optics in high demand for interconnect clusters of GPUs and memory. “There’s this gap between where the interconnect is and where compute is,” Sekerka said. “That really creates a problem for the system designer because you can’t exploit the advantages of the advances in compute.”

The transition from copper to optical typically involves moving to pluggable modules, but Sekerka said this form factor consumes a lot of power in modern AI clusters, which is why Ranovus has developed its own chip that can go into the module.

Like Ranovus, Dream Photonics is addressing opportunities created by the rapid growth of AI data centers. Company co-founder Sudip Shekhar said the bulk of the cost of a transceiver is the packaging, testing and assembly, not the photonic IC (PIC) itself. “This is a huge problem.”

He said the current generation of silicon photonics is in danger of running out of steam. “Building the next generation of transceivers is like building a rocket ship.”

Dream Photonics has been in business for five years and collaborating with 30 customers while taking inspiration from LEGO—the company’s hybrid integration aims to build transceivers with the best pieces available, including the lasers, modulators and detectors, Shekhar said.

The company’s 3D printed additive manufacturing lens solution can work with any foundry in the world. “You can put together different technologies and glue them together,” he said.

FABrIC funding is helping Dream Photonics with its go-to-market strategy with various component manufacturers to build the next generation of transceivers, Shekhar said.

AI is not the only driver of Canadian photonics technology development—it is also fueled by the need for quantum computing interconnects.

Montreal-based Aeponyx Enterprises was recently acquired by a neutral‑atom quantum computing company Pascal, leading the company to focus on developing systems based on laser technology and advancing their quantum computing capabilities by integrating its PICs directly into neutral‑atom processors, where arrays of laser beams trap, arrange and entangle atoms as qubits.

“The goal of the project is scale up the quantum computer,” Philippe Babin, Aeponyx’s general manager, said.

FABrIC funding will help Aeponyx develop a low-loss Si Nitride PIC platform to realize compact optical filters for refining the linewidth of semiconductor laser diodes operating at visible or NIR wavelengths for quantum computing applications.

Babin said Aeponyx will leverage CMC’s shared fabrication and packaging infrastructure to continue developing its PIC technology.

Pascal has strongly committed to keeping a presence in Montreal to further develop Aeponyx’s technology as it scales up its technology and delivers four quantum computers in Europe and Saudi Arabia, he said.  “The FABrIC project is basically meant to help us to develop the technology here in Canada.”

The second FABrIC funding challenge closed at the end of October and focused on attracting applications developing IoT products, including edge AI and ocean/marine IoT.

data.title: Canada Eyes AI Opportunities At The Edge
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When it comes to AI, Canada is a research trailblazer. But it has struggled to fully capitalize on its AI smarts.

At Accelerated, Canada’s Semiconductor Symposium held in late November, there was a lot of discussion around how the country can better capitalize on its AI science and talent to scale up its industry, especially around edge AI and sensor technology.

“Canada has a long tradition in research. We have a very strong talent pool,” said Garry Chan, chief AI officer at ventureLAB, during a presentation at the conference. The country also has some manufacturing capabilities, he said, but the challenge is how to bring it all together. “We have the people and resources. There’s a lot of challenges and things that you need to contend with as you scale.”

Chan said it is important to identify specific opportunities, perhaps around an industry such as mining. But getting the money necessary to scale is also critical, which may include government funding programs.

Canada’s federal government has made a slew of AI-related investment announcements in the past couple of years. For example, its 2025 budget included CDN $925.6 (U.S. $677 million) over five years to build large-scale sovereign public AI infrastructure to expand domestic AI compute and cloud capacity for public and private R&D. This funding is part of a broader CDN $2 billion (US $1.5 billion) Canadian Sovereign AI Compute Strategy, which targets AI, data center and computing initiatives to ensure secure, Canadian‑controlled AI resources.

Focusing on industries where “dual use” technologies can be developed is another strategy. Edge AI and sensor technology that can handle harsh environments, such as those in agriculture and marine applications, could also be hardy enough for defense—and vice versa.

Jean-Francois Bousquet, principal investigator at Dalhousie University’s Microelectronics Innovation, Design and Integration Hub located on Canada’s east coast, said marine surveillance and fish tracing applications presents opportunities for innovation around communications, sensing and processing. “It requires a whole lot of microelectronics.”

Bousquet said it is incumbent upon industry to clearly articulate to semiconductor companies what the real-world use cases are for sensor technologies, as well as the many considerations and costs. Creating this awareness will help forge the right partnerships, Bousquet said.

Calvin Cheng, director of Applications and Customer Success at Lumotive, said focusing on customer pain points and addressing them with a pilot is how his company differentiates itself in a competitive environment. The company develops solid-state optical beam steering chips and reference designs for 3D LiDAR sensors based on its proprietary Light Control Metasurface (LCM) technology that is used in 3D sensing systems for applications like industrial automation, robotics, automotive ADAS and smart infrastructure.

He said early adopters can help companies prove their technology in the marketplace, even if they are not a big customer. “This customer won’t be the major revenue generation customer for you going forward,” Cheng said, but they can help you find commonalities with other customers that are scaling up.

Pilots offer the opportunity to work out interoperability issues, which are critical for scaling up edge AI and IoT, as well as senor technologies.

Canada’s not-for-profit Centre of Excellence in Next Generation Networks (CENGN) provides advanced testbeds, living labs, and engineering support so startups and scaleups can test, validate, and commercialize networking, 5G/6G, AI, and IoT solutions.

Chris Joyce, VP of business development and marketing at CENGN, said CENGN test deployments typically represent real-world industrial environments where customers wan to adopt leading edge technologies. “We’re shepherding startups to stand up and demonstrate those technologies to meet the needs within those environments.”

He said many prototypes are built in siloed control environments that do not replicate and consider the complexities of real networks, such as multi-vendor interoperability, cloud edge integration, variability of radio conditions and real end user traffic patterns.

CENGN is set up so companies can test in environments that represent industrial settings where small and medium-sized enterprises, can test, validate and certify their technologies, Joyce said. “They’re shortening and development life cycles. They’re transitioning from proof of concept to operational readiness with increased confidence.”

Peter Wong, who heads up corporate strategy for STMicroelectronics out of Vancouver, said there is an opportunity to ride the coattails of the massive investment being made in LLMs and cloud infrastructure. “All that build up is great. You see more and more opportunities blow down.”

He said there are two areas in edge AI that are ripe with opportunities, including a lot of movement toward putting AI into sensors. “We’re certainly seeing a trend of pushing more and more intelligence right into the actual sensors.”

Wong said there is also a lot of effort being made to put inference capabilities in microcontrollers, and a lot of Canadian semiconductor content that can support the AI edge ecosystem and build on what is already rolled out.
